video_title: Michael Pettis: The Trade Reckoning Has Barely Begun | Global Macro | Ep.102
transcript: Uh, basically, if you think things have gotten bad in in the past couple of years, well, you ain't seen nothing yet because nothing has fundamentally changed. What really matters is if the US is able to get its act together and address its trade imbalances. And I think eventually it will. The US has to reindustrialize and as part of that, it must bring down its trade deficit. That's when things really start to get rough for Europe. >> [music] >> Welcome to Top Traders Unplugged. In markets, success doesn't come from predicting what happens next. It comes [music] from being prepared for what you can't predict. No noise, [music] no shortcuts, just real conversations to help you think better and invest [music] with confidence. >> Welcome back to Top Traders Unplugged. My name is Alan Dunn and this week I'm delighted to be joined by Michael Pettis. Michael is a senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace. The most recent one, Class Wars or Trade Wars with Matt Klein. Michael, great to have you on as a guest. How are you? >> Very good, thanks. >> Well, we as I mentioned, uh you're the author of Class Wars with Trade Wars with Matt Klein and we've had Matt on before um and that was uh a great conversation. So, I've been really looking forward to this one as well and I've followed your work for for some time. Um I mean, you have a very interesting background. You started off in Wall Street and then you've migrated into teaching and research. What got you interested in economics, markets, finance in the first place? >> Well, you know, when I was on Wall Street, I I I I was running fixed income trading and capital markets desks uh uh specializing in Latin America. I I I have a strong Spanish and Latin American background. Um and one of the things that it occurred to me, which occurs to everyone who spends a lot of time trading Latin America, is that the uh uh you really need to understand Latin American economic history to get a sense of where it is and where it's going. So, I spent a huge amount of time studying Latin American economic history, and from there I sort of became a uh economic and finance history junkie, and I and that's sort of led naturally into thinking about economics and uh and the and the development process. >> Very good. And you I'm I think I'm right in saying you you you're not formally trained in in economics in in a sense you don't have a PhD. You have an an MBA in finance. Is that right? >> Yes, I have degrees in finance, but not in economics. I I and you know, not to be not to be nasty, but that means I probably make fewer mistakes than than people with PhDs in economics economics. >> Well, that's that was my question. I mean, it is interesting. Some people say he's an economist or he's not. I mean, what qualifies somebody to be an economist? Obviously, you're you're you're a a well-known and well-regarded economist, I would say now, without without a PhD. But I mean, do you think that you you you probably think that gives you a an an advantage over people who are more classically trained? Is that fair? >> Well, yeah, I'm I'm very much a systems guy. And one of the things that really worries me about economics is that as you know, there's very little connection. Economics started as a description of the economy, but it sort of developed its own sort of uh uh processes that have been divorced from the real economy. It's almost a cliche to say that, uh, whether economists are right or wrong has almost no impact on the the credibility of what they're saying, on the prestige of what they're saying. So, I would argue when you have two systems with their own internal processes and no feedback mechanism between the two, the tendency that inevitable inevitably that they that they separate. So, I would argue is that academics academic economists do a very good job of describing a particular type of economy. Unfortunately, that economy doesn't exist, probably never has existed, and bears very little uh, relationship to the Anglophone economies, and even less relationship to the non-Anglophone economies, and are almost incompetent in dealing with something like the Chinese economy, which is a very different economy. >> Okay. I mean, the uh, economists will say their models are a simplification, but they can still draw reasonable inferences from their models, but I guess you're you you disagree with that. >> Well, you know, the Ptolemaic model was also a simplification, uh, but its basic assumptions were wrong. They didn't apply to the universe, so they ended up retarding the science of of astronomy. And, you know, I would say most economists when they talk about trade, whether they realize it or not, they're basing their work on models that assume balanced trade, um, and you know, for example, the trade the uh, the comparative advantage uh, model of David Ricardo's. Um, if you go through the arithmetic, you realize the arithmetic only works when trade is balanced. So, if you assume that trade is balanced, if your models assume that trade is balanced, that there is no intervention in currency markets, that's another fundamental assumption of most trade models. Um then it becomes very hard to take uh uh conclusions from that model and use them to describe the actual world of trade. And and and it's interesting because eventually, you know, if you hit someone on the head enough times, they'll start to feel the pain. And I think economists are finally starting to recognize that trade imbalances are a very deep problem and need to be resolved. But this is something that if you're a business person or an investor or a policy maker, uh it didn't it wasn't so hard for you to figure that out. It became pretty obvious pretty early on. Economists are just starting to recognize that the existing trading system that we have has real fundamental problems with it. >> Okay. And as I say, you wrote wrote a book called Class Wars or Trade Wars um with Michael uh with Matt Klein a few years ago. So and it just kind of sets out your framework for thinking about international trade and trade imbalances and trade conflicts. And I guess it is uh it maybe counterintuitive, maybe I don't know if it's fair to say, but it definitely brings a different perspective on the source of trade imbalances. So maybe if you could maybe in your words set out what what that is and what consensus get wrong gets wrong in trade imbalances. >> Well, you know, I I one of the things that that Matt and I have tried to do is to make sure that people understand we haven't really invented a new theory of trade. Nothing that we say is especially original. Had you gone back and read John Maynard Keynes or Joan Robinson or Ragnar Nurkse or Michael Kalecki, uh any of those guys, I don't think they would have said anything, you know, they would have heard anything particularly novel. A very very famous economist, uh probably the most famous economist in the world who hasn't yet won a Nobel Prize told me recently we had this big discussion of trade and and amazingly enough I was able to bring him around to my side even though he's famously very arrogant about people he disagrees with. He said, "You know what you know what the issue is? I finally understood the issue." cuz I've known him for a while. We've had many many many discussions. And he said economists typically think in terms of price effects because that's much easier to model. But you think in terms of income effects. And that had never occurred to me but I think he was absolutely right. I think when you think systemically you think in terms of income effects. So what Matt and I did in that book and that was really based on a previous book that I had published in 2013 called the Great Rebalancing is that I tried to argue that trade imbalances are the result of income imbalances, right? Um so that's why we said that's why we titled that the trade wars are class wars. When income is well distributed within a society total consumption plus total investment is able to absorb everything that's produced within the economy. It's only when you have very high levels of income inequality in which the average person retains a very low share of what he or she produces that you end up with problems of excess capacity and trade imbalances. And that's the main point of our book. If you see trade imbalances um the this almost certainly means that there have been significant income imbalances. >> Yeah, and just maybe to trace that through then so from the perspective of um you know the the the surplus the big surplus economies such as China and and Germany as you write about in the book what you're saying is basically because the the the consumption average wages are are are kept down and and consumption is not as strong as it could be. There surpluses arise to corporate sector and they tend to save more and export those surpluses. Uh And and and and I guess the the counter fact of those capital can surpluses has to be trade deficit elsewhere. It's not very hard to say. >> Yeah, in fact the the cleanest example of this, the most obvious example of this was Europe itself. Because remember when the Euro was created in 2000 2001, countries like Spain, Italy, Portugal, Greece, France, some of the other so-called peripherals including Ireland, um were in reasonably good shape. They tended to run either small surpluses or small deficits. Some of them like Spain even ran fiscal surpluses. They tend None of them tended to have debt problems. They didn't have a real estate or asset price bubbles. And then all of that changed around 2003 2004. Suddenly the the Portuguese, the Italians, the Irish, the Spanish, the the Greeks went crazy, um started consuming like crazy, uh running into huge amounts of debt, encouraging massive real estate bubbles. And it's really an astonishing story. Why would all of these countries, very very different countries, all go crazy at exactly the same time? And interestingly enough, they all went crazy at exactly the same time that Germany started saving a huge amount of money and running massive current account surpluses, which is really convenient, right? Because the the the silliness of the peripheral Europeans matched the thriftiness of the of the Germans. And I would say there's no coincidence there at all. It was very clear what happened. In 2003-2004, Germany implemented the so-called Hartz reforms, the labor reforms, which basically pushed down wage growth. So, before then, wages in Germany were growing in line with productivity. After the Hartz reforms, productivity continued to grow a little bit more slowly, but wages stopped growing altogether. And instead, what ended up happening is business profits surged. Business profits, of course, are saved, whereas a worker's income is mostly consumed. So, the result was the Germans suddenly became thrifty. The German saving rate soared. Uh even if you look at household savings, they didn't go up, they went down. But the story developed that the Germans were extremely thrifty, and their thriftiness found its counterpart in the lack of thrift in Spain, Ireland, uh Italy, uh etc. And what I would argue is that that's not what happened at all. What happened in Ger- in Germany, very clearly, was savings went up not because of thriftiness, but because of labor policies that repressed the growth rate of of uh of uh wages, and so repressed consumption and forced up domestic saving. That saving had to go somewhere, and because of the creation of the euro, um it was very conveniently redeployed within Europe. And interestingly enough, all the countries that entered the euro with high inflation rates were the ones that received all of the German inflows, and that makes sense because as interest rates converged across Europe, they became real interest rates became negative in in in the former and and positive in countries like Germany. So, money poured into a country like Spain. I'll call it Spain rather than all the others to make it easier. But if money pours into Spain, if there's a net inflow of saving from Germany into Spain, then that change of Spain's external account, which must change its domestic imbalances, which means that if there is a net inflow of a hundred euros into Spain, we must see a gap between Spanish investment and Spanish saving. Now, Spain were a developing country with huge investment needs, perhaps that might have resulted in a surge in investment, in which case Germany's surpluses would have contributed to global growth. But like most advanced economies, uh Spanish uh investment is constrained not by the lack of saving, but by the lack of demand. So, investment didn't go up. There was a bit of an increase in investment in Spain in infrastructure. Spain has very good transportation infrastructure. Uh part of it went into the property bubble. Uh and of course in Ireland, you're very familiar with that. But very little of the net inflow resulted in an increase in um in uh investment. And so therefore, there had to be some other shift in the Spanish economy that caused unemployment to go down. And as we argue in the book, there's basically I'm sorry, that caused saving to go down. There's basically three ways saving can go down. The classical way described by Joan Robinson, who was one of uh Keynes's uh friends and disciples, she explained that um these inflows would drive up unemployment. And as you know, an unemployed worker has a negative saving rate. So, Spain adjusts that way. Higher unemployment, lower saving, everything balances. Of course, in the modern economy, we don't want unemployment to go up, and we have tools to prevent it. One tool is to encourage uh household lending, so you lower lending standards, and households borrow more, and of course debt is negative saving. And the other way is to increase the fiscal deficit. And of course, the fiscal deficit is never is is a negative saving. So, notice what happened. Changes in the distribution of income in Germany favoring businesses and the rich rather than workers caused a surge in the saving rate, which was matched by a decline in the saving rate in these other countries. And at first, the decline occurred because of a surge in debt, particularly household debt in countries like Spain and Ireland. And then later on, by 2009, when debt could no longer go up, it was balanced by a surge in unemployment. So, the purpose of the book is our is to argue that either there was a whole bunch of very surprising and very lucky coincidences, or you can you can treat the European imbalances as uh changes in the distribution of income driven particularly by policies in Germany. >> Yeah, it's I mean, it's very interesting. And as you say, it kind of mechanically has to be the case. I mean, in terms of the flows, the capital account has to balance, current account, etc. As well there are ways I mean, a couple of questions um One, I mean, it it's kind of like the thinking at the time in Germany was we have to boost productivity, we have to boost competitiveness. You know, it's all about competitiveness. Better competitiveness will mean more jobs, better for everybody. And I've just started reading reading Wolfgang Münchau's book about Germany. And he it's very much aligned with your thinking that this kind of flawed thinking about, you know, fiscal responsibility, keeping your fiscal house in order, you know, it is consistent with your point. I suppose my question is was it a conscious decision to favor corporates, to favor the elites, or was it misguided thinking? >> Uh well, I'm glad you I'm glad you put it that way because you said the Germans decided that they needed to increase productivity and competitiveness. And what I would argue is that those are two completely separate things. First of all, com- productivity didn't grow after the Hartz reforms. It grew more slowly than before the Hartz reforms. So, if that was the point, they were clearly a failure. But competitiveness went up significantly, right? And why did German competitiveness go up even when productivity didn't? Because German wages went down. And this was the so-called Kalecki paradox, right? Kalecki argued that you can improve your profits if you lower your wages, but if we all lower our wages, our profits will not all go up. In fact, they'll go down. And the reason is because it's wages that drive demand and wages that drive productivity improvements. So, I would say that lowering wages is not a way of increasing productivity. It actually reduces productivity, but it increases competitiveness. And that's the point. Um so, what I what ended up happening with Germany is it became not more productive, but much more competitive, and that caused enormous losses in Germany's trading partners. And that's what we see at the global level, right? So, if you want to increase productivity, uh that's a very, very different issue. And and and when I go to Europe and speak to EU officials, I try to make this point as strongly as I can. Europe, you have a competitiveness problem, not as big as the US. The US has a huge competitive competitiveness problem, and you can see that in the size of the trade deficit. And you also have a productivity problem, which the US doesn't have. US has pretty good productivity growth, and these are very separate things. If you want to improve productivity, there are all kind of things you can do, but if you want to improve competitiveness, there's only two things you can do. One is to match countries like China, which have very low unit labor costs, right? Not low wages, low unit labor costs, low wages relative to productivity. So, you can wipe out your social welfare system, you can lower wages, you can do all of these things that'll make you much more competitive. Is that a good thing? No, not really, because as you lower wages, not only will you have political problems, but more importantly, your contribution to global growth will go down, your share of global growth will go up, and that's the Kalecki paradox, right? If you cut wages, you benefit, but the world is worse off. And if we all cut wages, nobody benefits, we're all worse off. So, one thing you can do, Europe, is cut wages, right? Eliminate your social safety net. I think that's a terrible idea, but if you want to do that to regain competitiveness, that'll work. The only other thing you can do is to intervene in your external account, right? And since you can't really intervene in your currency, then you have to do what countries do when they can't intervene in their currency, and that is to implement tariffs. But if you were going to implement tariffs, I would say there, you have to make very sure you don't do it the way the US did it. Bilateral tariffs, sectoral tariffs are a waste of time. The purpose of tariffs are really to replicate a currency devaluation if you can't do a currency devaluation. >> I mean, to some extent just get into that, but I mean, just on the general framework, I mean, you're very much pinning it on the German policy choice of in the hearts reforms 2003 being the primary driver. Now, equally at the time, obviously, we had a weak German economy, so European interest rates were obviously targeted at the Eurozone in general, so that meant rates were probably lower than ideal for Ireland and Spain. And obviously, we did have a housing boom here, and we, you know, we lived through it. We did go crazy buying property and spending. How can you say like that side of it is the primary driver? The the the German capital coming our way is a bigger driver than our our own consumption, our own property boom, or or is it just coincident? Or how do you reconcile those? >> Well, if there were a special Irish thing about property bubbles, I would say, "Okay, it was probably your fault." But there isn't. Ireland has no more property bubbles than anybody else. And what's really striking is that you guys went crazy about property at the same time everyone else did. And so, I've really trouble believing that that's domestic. So, for example, you know, again, I was born in Spain, my family owns businesses there, I know Spain very well. And in the 1990s, you know, if you had a credit card, you would sort of brag about it. It was quite hard to get a credit card. But by the 2000s, you know, if you didn't have 12 credit cards, it's cuz you weren't trying. Um what ended up happening is that Spanish banks significantly lowered their their their lending standards because of massive inflows from Germany that had to be recycled. And I suspect the same thing happened in in Europe. I'm sorry, in Ireland. Something very similar happened in in in the United States because in part because of mortgage securitization, massive amounts of capital flowed into the mortgage industry. And by now, it's pretty well documented the way American banks responded was by lowering their lending standards. They used to have very strict lending standards and they eliminated them, basically eliminated them. The famous ninja loans, you know, no income, no job, no assets. You could still borrow money and buy property. And I would argue that that's really what drives the process. It was changes in the underlying liquidity of the banking system, which were driven, I would argue, by by the flow of capital from Germany into countries like Ireland. >> And back to the point, I mean, do you think it was just misguided policy or or or or a policy choice to favor the corporate sector? >> Well, you know, Joan Robinson wrote about this in 1937 in her famous essay in which she introduced the phrase beggar thy neighbor. If you want to be technical, she said beggar my neighbor, but that sort of evolved into beggar thy neighbor. And she said the point of beggar thy neighbor policies is so that you can export the consequences of domestic policies to your trade partners. So, if you have an unemployment problem and you want to boost employment, one of the ways of doing so is by reducing wages. The problem with reducing wages in a closed system is that if you reduce wages, you reduce demand. Right? But in an open system, if you reduce wages, you can increase your trade surplus and therefore you don't suffer the unemployment consequences of lower wages. You pass them on to your trade partners. So, I would say what Germany did was perfectly rational for Germany. Beggar thy neighbor policies are perfectly rational, but they're bad for the global system, which is why they should be prevented, which is why Keynes' proposal at Bretton Woods insisted that countries not be allowed to run large persistent trade surpluses. >> Yeah. So, this is an idea that's been around a while. You you referenced the economist Hobson from 1900s. And I mean I mean, everything you're saying makes sense, but it's counterintuitive to everything that I learned in economics. I I don't remember any economics lecture when somebody said, "Let's boost consumption to boost growth." It was all about, "Let's reduce real wages to solve the unemployment problem." Where did it get lost along the way? And why is this uh counter to the to the orthodoxy? >> Well, I would argue that we took a wrong turn in the 1970s and 1980s. We because before this this was quite well understood. You know, there's there's Say's law, uh which is that there's no such thing as insufficient demand. Um but Say's law operated in the economy in which there was unlimited investment needs. And he really didn't understand an economy in which investment could be constrained by the lack of demand. Once investment is constrained by the lack of demand, then demand becomes very important. This is one of Keynes's big insights. Demand really does uh does matter. In uh in I think January or February, the ECB came out with a report, which I think is pretty important. They asked They asked European businesses, "Why aren't you investing more? Why aren't you expanding capacity? Why aren't you investing in productivity-enhancing technology?" And they didn't say, "It's because we lack saving. It's because capital is too expensive." They said, "It's because there's no demand." That was the number one reason. The number two reason, which is basically the same thing, is because it's not profitable. Uh which is just another way of saying there's no demand. So, if you really want to increase business investment, investment in productive capacity, either you have to increase demand for that capacity domestically, or you have to run trade surpluses basically to take demand away from your trade partners. The latter is a problem. And again, Keynes was very much opposed to this. He said that you shouldn't be able to grow off the back of demand of your trade partners. It should be domestic demand, which means domestic wages, which should drive growth. But we're we live in a globalized system in which it makes a lot of sense to play the Kalecki game uh to grow faster by uh uh by taking demand from your trade partners. And you do that by lowering your your unit labor costs. It's not a coincidence that China has the lowest unit labor cost perhaps of any economy in the world, certainly of any meaningful economy, and it has the highest trade surplus. That's not an accident. >> [music] >> Moving to China, obviously, this has been a policy choice, and it's kind of been this neo-mercantilist [music] approach um for many years. From reading your work, you seem to be quite pessimistic on things changing in China. There is um I suppose a government bias towards keeping um uh consumption levels below where they maybe should be and and focusing on on investment and um manufacturing. Talk to us a little bit about the structure of the economy. How is it set up that way? >> Well, I'm pessimistic about China precisely because it was not a choice. Um another economist that I mention a lot, Albert Hirschman, pointed out way back in the 1970s that very successful growth models tend to end up with very very difficult adjustments. And he argued that a successful growth model, by definition, ends up no longer being a successful growth model. Because what's a successful growth model? It's one that identifies your particular problems and solves them. But once your problems are solved, that model no longer has any validity. It's no longer a good model. And Hirschman's question was why is it that no one ever changes to a new model? And what he argued is that because a successful growth model creates political, legal, economic, financial institutions that depend on that model, and they become disproportionately powerful. And it becomes very difficult to shift to the to a very different model once the model has run its course. So, I would argue what happened in China, which very much is what happened in in in Brazil in the '50s and '60s, uh in the Soviet Union during that same period, and in Japan in the '70s and '80s, and at least a dozen other countries, is that they started this process hugely under underinvested. So, Japan was underinvested because it was basically destroyed by war. Uh China was underinvested because it had gone through five decades of anti-Japanese war, followed by civil war, followed by Maoism. The Soviet Union also destroyed by war. It's a pretty common pattern. And what these countries needed more than anything else was a lot of investment to rebuild infrastructure, rebuild manufacturing capacity, rebuild housing. And so, they put into place very powerful growth models that did exactly that. So, when you have very high investment rates, those investments have to be funded by saving. And typically, you import saving from abroad. So, the United States in the 19th century was a huge net importer of foreign capital to fund its very high investment. But uh uh depending on foreign capital is very risky. Every time England had a cold, the United States had a depression. And so, a model that that was really developed in the 1930s by by Germany and the Soviet Union, both pariah nations who couldn't borrow from abroad, was to fund high investment by forcing up domestic saving. And the basic way you force up domestic saving is you suppress consumption. And the easiest way to suppress consumption is by suppressing wages. And they did this very successfully. Uh and what ended up happening in China was wages uh were so low. They started in the 1980s at a reg- you know, at a relatively normal level, but by 2000, they had uh declined as a share of GDP to among the lowest in the world. And by 2011, they had declined to a share that was totally surreal. We've never seen such a low number. Now, was this a bad thing? No, not necessarily, because when you reduce the wage share of GDP, you force up saving. And if the saving pours into productive investment, you end up with such rapid growth that wages also grow rapidly. They grow more slowly relative to GDP, but when China was growing at 10%, wages were growing at 7%, right? More slowly, but still a great a great rate of of growth. So, this was a great model. The problem is that it can only be sustained when you have very high investment needs relative to your actual investment. And China probably closed the gap uh by the late 2000. Japan probably closed the gap by the early 1980s. But, it was very very difficult for them to switch, to change their growth models. So, they ended up maintaining these models that suppressed wage growth. And that really subsidized investment across the board. So, China has gone from one investment bubble to another. In the early 2010s, in the late 2010s, it was the property investment, which as you know, uh collapsed in 2022. For the next 4 years, it was a manufacturing investment bubble, which led to the problems of involution. And so, now it's switching back to an infrastructure investment bubble. But, they just go from bubble to bubble, and it's not because they want to run surpluses out of any, you know, vicious uh uh a tendency. It's because it's extremely difficult to change the model without accepting a significant slowdown in growth and a significant reduction in their share of global manufacturing. And as long as they can't accept those two things, they cannot rebalance. >> Yeah. I mean, if we go back to maybe 2000 and China was came into WTO, um you know, the view in the West was, okay, China is going to be a manu you know, manufacturing powerhouse, but you know, manufacturing growth will will fuel rising living living standards. Um eventually, you'll have a more service-oriented economy. It the Chinese market will be a big market for for the West. Uh and and probably parallel to that with with rising wealth will come uh more demands for democracy, etc. And China will come into to the world system. Obviously, that hasn't been the case. Was that thinking uh reasonable at the time, or was that a kind of a flawed idea in the first place? >> No, it was pretty silly. It's the type of reasoning that comes from people who haven't bothered reading the history. You know, China didn't invent this model. We've seen this model many times before. And it's always run into the same problems. The The only interesting question is whether whether the adjustment is really quick and brutal or you know, socially and politically brutal or is it very slow, drawn-out, and and economically much worse, but spread out over a long period of time? Uh so, you can say uh the the American adjustment in the 1930s, the US, which had similar problems, adjusted very quickly and brutally, or the Japanese adjustment, which took, you know, 20-30 years, depending on when you believe it ended, which was much worse economically, but much better politically and socially. But ultimately, every single case of an investment-driven growth miracle has ended in a difficult adjustment. So, to assume that this time would be different really doesn't make a lot of sense, but but that's what we all assumed. >> Yeah. Okay, I mean, taking us to today, and obviously China still has a huge trade surplus. Um I mean, obviously, you could say the US is resisting that with its tariffs. In Europe, it's a big issue. You know, we're seeing more EVs coming in, solar, etc. So, it presents a big dilemma from from the European perspective, and obviously, Germany initially benefited from that, the metal standard as as kind of supplier of capital goods to China. That's all changed now. I mean, who will absorb China's trade surplus, do you think, going forward? >> Well, you know, this may surprise people, but nothing has really changed in the sense that um most of the surpluses of the world continue to be Chinese, roughly half. Uh but most of the deficits of the world, uh again, roughly half, continue to be American. And if you throw in uh two countries with very similar financial markets, uh Canada and the UK, those three countries together account for 70 to 80% of global deficits, right? So, what we've seen is a shifting around of trade patterns, but we haven't really seen an adjustment yet. And in fact, this is what I tell my friends in in Europe. Uh basically, if you think things have gotten bad in in the past couple of years, well, you ain't seen nothing yet, because nothing has fundamentally changed. What really matters is if the US is able to get its act together and address its trade imbalances. And I think eventually it will. It's very hard, you know, to shift from one model to another, but I think there is a strong bipartisan consensus in the United States that the US has to reindustrialize, and as part of that, it must bring down its trade deficit. That's when things really start to get rough for Europe. Because right now, uh the Chinese surplus with the US has gone down, but the Chinese surpluses for with the rest of the world, including Europe, has gone up. But meanwhile, the US deficit with China, which has gone down, has been more than matched by a rising US deficit with the rest of the world, including Europe. So, what ends up happening is that although China is not running huge imbalances with the US, US imbalances are nonetheless funding Chinese imbalances. If the US is able to bring down its deficit, then the world faces a simple arithmetic problem, because if the US is more than 50% of global deficits, and the US, the UK, and Canada are more than 70% of global deficits, then if the US is able to bring down its deficit, probably the US and the UK and Canada will be forced to do the same, in which case that leaves us with a problem. Either the surpluses of the manufacturing surplus countries have to come down just as quickly, which would be brutally difficult not just for China, but also for um Taiwan, South Korea, Sweden, probably Germany. Or somebody else must replace the US as the big deficit country. And given the type of uh manufacturing which is uh which we're increasingly seeing in China and which will be part of the manufacturing revival in the US, it cannot be the developing world that ends up replacing the US as the big deficit source.
